title: Brewing Water Adjustment Workflow
description: Step-by-step decision workflow for adjusting tap or well water to match a target brewing water profile.

inputs.label: Starting Water
inputs.description: What type of water are you starting with?

inputs.label: Beer Style
inputs.description: What style of beer are you brewing?

steps.description: Review your water report. Note calcium, sulfate, chloride, sodium, magnesium, and bicarbonate/alkalinity levels. Proceed to step 4.
Get your water tested (e.g., Ward Labs) or treat your water as RO/distilled to build from scratch. For immediate brewing, use RO or distilled water.
Compare your starting water to your target profile. Identify which ions are too high, too low, or need balancing.
Starting from RO/distilled: all ions are near zero. Proceed directly to mineral additions.

Reduce bicarbonate if needed. Options: (a) dilute with RO water to reduce all minerals proportionally, (b) acidify with phosphoric or lactic acid to neutralize bicarbonate, (c) boil the water and decant (reduces temporary hardness).

Select a target water profile for your beer style. For hoppy beers, prioritize sulfate:chloride ratio >1.5:1. For malty beers, prioritize chloride >= sulfate. For Pilsners, target very low mineral content.

Calculate mineral additions needed to reach your target. Use a brewing water calculator or the mineral chart. Common additions: Gypsum (Ca + SO₄), Calcium Chloride (Ca + Cl), Epsom Salt (Mg + SO₄), Baking Soda (Na + HCO₃).

Add minerals to your water. For most minerals, add to the mash water before doughing in. Chalk (CaCO₃) should be added directly to the mash. Baking soda can be added to sparge water to prevent over-acidification.
Measure mash pH 5–10 minutes after doughing in. Target 5.2–5.4 for most styles.
pH too high. Add lactic acid (1–2 mL per 5 gallons) or phosphoric acid. Wait 5 minutes and re-measure. Repeat as needed.
pH too low. Add small amounts of calcium carbonate (chalk) or baking soda. Wait 5 minutes and re-measure. Note: very dark malts can drive pH low — this is expected for styles like stout.
pH is in acceptable range. Proceed with your mash. Record your mineral additions and final pH for future reference.

outputs.label: Mineral Additions
outputs.description: Grams of each mineral to add
